chain_of_thought: Tools are unreachable, so I must reason from the high-level crash info only.
Crash is in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum within libUbikeyJni.so, called via Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* and CERT_Load from Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID.
The function names clearly indicate ASN.1/X.509 certificate parsing; Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum likely walks ASN.1 structures and counts children of a node.

Fuzzer supplies arbitrary byte[] from Java through UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID into native code with no evidence of validation, mimicking attacker-controlled certificate data in real use (e.g., untrusted certs, OCR output, or remote inputs).

Process termination at Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, not in sanitizer/allocator, suggests internal logic error (likely bounds miscalculation, length misinterpretation, or unchecked recursion) on malformed ASN.1.

Such logic in certificate parsers is historically prone to out-of-bounds reads/writes, stack exhaustion, or infinite recursion, and function name suggests computation of child count without explicit bounds checking.
However, we lack exact decompilation or stack details (no SIGSEGV vs abort message), so we cannot prove OOB or memory corruption; could also be a defensive abort on malformed input (DoS only).
Given fuzzing context, arbitrary bytes cause native crash instead of graceful error, which is at least a denial-of-service if reachable with attacker-controlled cert input.
JNI callchain shows byte[] comes from app code (zo1.j -> h63.a -> various handlers -> UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID) with no visible validation, so attacker-controllable length/content is plausible.

Thus, I classify as a likely vulnerability (DoS / potential memory safety) with medium severity and moderate confidence, but I cannot specify an exact CWE beyond generic improper input validation; out-of-bounds (CWE-787) is plausible but not proven.
reasons: Crash occurs in ASN.1/X.509 parsing routine Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum within app-native libUbikeyJni.so, not in the fuzz harness or allocators.

JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID accepts arbitrary byte[] from Java; call graph shows it is passed through multiple layers without clear validation, implying attacker-controlled ASN.1 input is possible.
Malformed input causes process termination instead of clean error handling, representing at least a denial-of-service and suggesting fragile parsing logic that may conceal memory-safety issues.

evidence.note: Top native frame at crash, part of ASN.1/X.509 certificate parsing chain called from CERT_Load and JNI jGetPolicyOID.
JNI bridge taking a byte[] from Java, forwarding to CERT_Load and ASN.1/X.509 parsing without observed pre-validation in the provided call graph.
Java method is reachable from application handlers that operate on arbitrary byte arrays; no constraints shown in call graph, making input plausibly attacker-controlled.

recommendations: Manually reverse and audit CERT_Load, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* and Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum to identify exact failure conditions (bounds checks, recursion limits, length parsing).
Harden ASN.1 parsing: validate all length fields and child counts before use, cap recursion depth, and ensure functions return errors instead of aborting on malformed input.
Add fuzz-derived regression tests for jGetPolicyOID with malformed certificates and ensure JNI/native layer returns a Java exception or null instead of crashing.
assumptions: Byte[] argument to UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID is at least partially controllable by an attacker in realistic app scenarios (e.g., untrusted certificate or OCR-derived data).
Process termination in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is not solely due to the fuzz harness doing something impossible in production (e.g., violating hidden preconditions).
limitations: Ghidra MCP access failed, so there is no direct decompiled view of Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum or JNI glue code.
Exact crash type (SIGSEGV vs abort vs assert) and register/memory state at crash are not provided, limiting precise CWE classification.
Java-side code around com.quram.mi.ocr.* callers was not decompiled, so attacker-controllability of the byte[] argument is inferred from signatures and call graph only.

chain_of_thought: Tools are unavailable (connection refused), so I must reason from symbols, stack, and Java call graph only.
The crash is in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → Get_DER_Child → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID.
These functions are all in libUbikeyJni.so, which suggests custom ASN.1 / X.509 parsing code inside the app’s native library, not a system library.
__memcpy as top frame with ASN.1 parsing below strongly hints at an out-of-bounds memory access (read or write) during DER decoding, typical of unsafe length handling in certificate/ASN.1 decoders.

JNI bridge jGetPolicyOID([B)Ljava/lang/String; takes a raw byte[] from Java and passes it to CERT_Load / ASN.1 decoder with no evidence of validation from the provided Java callgraph; h63.a and the other handlers are generic wrappers and likely do not enforce strict DER structure/length limits.

The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input injects arbitrary data into that byte[], showing that the crash is directly influenced by attacker-controlled input, and there is no indication that production code would structurally sanitize the array before passing it down.

Since the memcpy failure occurs inside Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, which is logically part of parsing the supplied DER, it is highly likely that the function trusts internal length fields / offsets from the attacker-controlled DER without robust bounds checks, leading to overflow or out-of-bounds read/write.

Such a bug in X.509/ASN.1 decoding of untrusted input is a classic memory-corruption vulnerability (CWE-787 / CWE-125) with potential for RCE, especially when using memcpy on heap buffers whose sizes are derived from parsed lengths.

Nothing in the stack suggests this is a sanitizer-only abort or a deliberate harness-only failure; instead, it is the allocator / libc (__memcpy) reacting to bad pointers/lengths emerging from app-level parsing logic.

Given the clear attacker control (input byte[]), proximity of the unsafe operation (memcpy) in ASN.1 decode, and typical exploitability of such bugs, I classify this as a genuine, likely exploitable vulnerability, though exact severity may be high rather than critical since we lack precise write vs read confirmation.
Confidence is high (0.9) based on standard patterns, but not 1.0 since I cannot decompile and inspect exact bounds checks due to MCP tool failure.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy invoked from custom ASN.1/X.509 decoding functions in libUbikeyJni.so, indicating unsafe buffer copy during parsing.
JNI method jGetPolicyOID([B) receives attacker-controlled byte[] and forwards it into CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST without visible structural validation in provided callgraph.
Pattern matches classic out-of-bounds memory access in DER/ASN.1 length/offset handling (likely CWE-787/CWE-125), not a harness-only or benign abort.

evidence.note: Top application-level frame under __memcpy during DER ASN.1 decoding of attacker-controlled data.
JNI entry takes Java byte[] argument and passes it into CERT_Load/ASN.1 decoder with no shown size or structure validation.
Java → JNI bridge allows arbitrary byte[] to reach native ASN.1/X.509 parser as per callgraph from com.quram.mi.ocr.zo1.j.

recommendations: Review and harden all ASN.1/X.509 decoding paths in libUbikeyJni.so, ensuring strict bounds checks before memcpy/memmove and rejecting malformed length/offset fields.
Add explicit validation in Java and JNI for the input byte[] (e.g., size limits, DER sanity checks) before passing it to CERT_Load / ASN.1 parsing routines.
Instrument the native code with sanitizers (ASan/UBSan) and targeted tests on malformed certificates to pinpoint the exact memcpy call and fix out-of-bounds conditions.
assumptions: The Java-side callers do not fully sanitize or constrain the content/structure of the byte[] beyond what is shown in the callgraph.
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and siblings directly interpret length/offset fields from the supplied DER data without comprehensive bounds checks.
The crash is due to an invalid pointer/length in memcpy rather than an unrelated platform or harness fault.
limitations: Could not decompile libUbikeyJni.so or Java classes due to MCP connection failure; reasoning is based on symbol names and stack only.
Exact nature of the memory violation (out-of-bounds read vs write) and precise controllability of offsets/lengths cannot be confirmed.
No visibility into any internal defensive checks that might reduce exploitability, such as capped lengths or structured validation.
